---
title: "Lesson 1: A Confidence Interval for Proportions"
subtitle: "Statistical Estimation for Data Science and AI"
---

::: {.column-margin}
{{< video 01_a-confidence-interval-for-proportions.mp4 >}}

[Transcript](01_a-confidence-interval-for-proportions.en.txt)
[Handout](02_video-slides-for-a-confidence-interval-for-proportions_module5_lesson1.pdf){width=700 height=980}
:::

## A CI for a Proportion {#sec-ci-proportion}

::: {.callout-note}
## Critical-value notation

Throughout these notes, $z_\gamma$ denotes the positive standard-normal critical value satisfying

$$
P(Z > z_\gamma)=\gamma
$$

Thus, for a 95% confidence interval, $z_{0.025}=1.96$
:::

::: {#exm-election-proportion}
## Election Poll

A random sample of 500 people in a country that is about to hold a national election were asked whether they preferred Candidate A or Candidate B. Of those sampled, 320 preferred Candidate A.

Construct an approximate 95% confidence interval for the true proportion of people in the country who prefer Candidate A.
:::

Let $p$ be the true population proportion who prefer Candidate A. The sample proportion is

$$
\widehat{p}
=
\frac{320}{500}
=
\frac{16}{25}
=
0.64
$$

In general, the estimator is

$$
\widehat{p}
=
\frac{\text{number in the sample with the characteristic}}{\text{sample size}}
$$

### The Bernoulli Model {#sec-bernoulli-model-proportion}

Take a random sample of size $n$ and define

$$
X_i
=
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{person }i\text{ prefers Candidate A} \\
0 & \text{person }i\text{ prefers Candidate B}
\end{cases}
$$

Then

$$
X_1,X_2,\ldots,X_n
\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}
\mathcal{Bern}(p)
$$

The sample proportion is the sample mean of these indicator variables:

$$
\widehat{p}
=
\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}X_i}{n}
=
\overline{X}
$$

Its expectation and variance are

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[\widehat{p}]
&=
\mathbb{E}[\overline{X}] \\
&=
\mathbb{E}[X_1] \\
&=
p
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{Var}(\widehat{p})
&=
\operatorname{Var}(\overline{X}) \\
&=
\frac{\operatorname{Var}(X_1)}{n} \\
&=
\frac{p(1-p)}{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

By the Central Limit Theorem,

$$
\widehat{p}
\overset{\mathrm{approx}}{\sim}
\mathcal{N}\!\left(
 p,
 \frac{p(1-p)}{n}
\right)
$$

Equivalently, as $n$ becomes large,

$$
\frac{\widehat{p}-p}
{\sqrt{p(1-p)/n}}
\overset{\mathrm{approx}}{\sim}
\mathcal{N}(0,1)
$$

### When Is the Normal Approximation Reasonable? {#sec-normal-approximation-proportion}

The rule $n>30$ is a generic heuristic, but the Bernoulli model permits a more relevant check. The estimator $\widehat{p}$ must lie in $[0,1]$, whereas a normal random variable has support on the entire real line.

Since approximately 99.7% of a normal distribution lies within three standard deviations of its mean, proceed with the normal approximation when

$$
\left(
\widehat{p}
-
3\sqrt{\frac{\widehat{p}(1-\widehat{p})}{n}},
\widehat{p}
+
3\sqrt{\frac{\widehat{p}(1-\widehat{p})}{n}}
\right)
\subseteq [0,1]
$$

The true standard deviation of $\widehat{p}$ is

$$
\sigma_{\widehat{p}}
=
\sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}
$$

Because $p$ is unknown, estimate it by

$$
\widehat{\sigma}_{\widehat{p}}
=
\sqrt{\frac{\widehat{p}(1-\widehat{p})}{n}}
$$

### Constructing the Confidence Interval {#sec-construct-ci-proportion}

A central $100(1-\alpha)\%$ probability statement is

$$
-z_{\alpha/2}
<
\frac{\widehat{p}-p}
{\sqrt{p(1-p)/n}}
<
z_{\alpha/2}
$$

Although $p$ can be isolated exactly, doing so requires solving the quadratic inequality

$$
(\widehat{p}-p)^2
-
z_{\alpha/2}^{2}\frac{p(1-p)}{n}
<
0
$$

The more common large-sample approximation replaces $p$ in the standard error by $\widehat{p}$.

::: {#def-wald-ci-proportion}
## Approximate Confidence Interval for a Proportion

An approximate $100(1-\alpha)\%$ confidence interval for $p$ is

$$
\boxed{
\widehat{p}
\pm
z_{\alpha/2}
\sqrt{\frac{\widehat{p}(1-\widehat{p})}{n}}
}
$$
:::

### Returning to the Election Poll {#sec-election-proportion-solution}

Here,

$$
n=500
\qquad
\widehat{p}=\frac{16}{25}=0.64
$$

The three-standard-error check gives

$$
\begin{aligned}
\widehat{p}
\pm
3\sqrt{\frac{\widehat{p}(1-\widehat{p})}{n}}
&=
0.64
\pm
3\sqrt{\frac{0.64(0.36)}{500}} \\
&=
0.64\pm 0.0644 \\
&=
(0.5756,0.7044)
\end{aligned}
$$

This interval is contained in $[0,1]$, so the normal approximation is reasonable.

For a 95% confidence interval, $z_{0.025}=1.96$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\widehat{p}
\pm
z_{0.025}
\sqrt{\frac{\widehat{p}(1-\widehat{p})}{n}}
&=
0.64
\pm
1.96\sqrt{\frac{0.64(0.36)}{500}} \\
&=
0.64\pm 0.0421 \\
&=
\boxed{(0.5979,0.6821)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## Reflection
